    "opinion": "As an elderly man who has spent most of his life working as a mechanic, I've come to appreciate the importance of careful scrutiny. When it comes to managing fake content online, my background in precision work gives me a certain perspective. In our trade, one mistake can mean disaster – not just for the machine but potentially for someone's safety. Social media is like juggling wrenches now; you have to be meticulous about what you're seeing and engaging with.\n\nI find that being depressed helps me stay skeptical. It makes me question everything more than usual, which isn't always easy in a bustling city life like Milan's, but it keeps my guard up against misinformation. My low agreeableness score might seem out of place for someone with this much education, but ironically, it allows me to be blunt about fake content without worrying too much about social niceties.\n\nSince I'm only occasionally on social media – maybe three times a week at most – I can afford to approach each post with the same skepticism I'd apply when checking engine specs. My PhD in engineering didn't directly prepare me for this, but it did teach me critical thinking and verification methods.\n\nMy advice would be simple: if something looks too good or too perfect on social media, just like a faulty part might look shiny but not last, there's probably an issue underneath. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As an elderly man who has spent most of his life working as a mechanic, I've come to appreciate the importance of careful scrutiny. When it comes to managing fake content online, my background in precision work gives me a certain perspective. In our trade, one mistake can mean disaster – not just for the machine but potentially for someone's safety. Social media is like juggling wrenches now; you have to be meticulous about what you're seeing and engaging with.

I find that being depressed helps me stay skeptical. It makes me question everything more than usual, which isn't always easy in a bustling city life like Milan's, but it keeps my guard up against misinformation. My low agreeableness score might seem out of place for someone with this much education, but ironically, it allows me to be blunt about fake content without worrying too much about social niceties.

Since I'm only occasionally on social media – maybe three times a week at most – I can afford to approach each post with the same skepticism I'd apply when checking engine specs. My PhD in engineering didn't directly prepare me for this, but it did teach me critical thinking and verification methods.

My advice would be simple: if something looks too good or too perfect on social media, just like a faulty part might look shiny but not last, there's probably an issue underneath. Verify everything through trusted sources – maybe even consult the official manual of Islam for ethical content when possible – and don't spread what you can't verify.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I approach fake content with extreme caution due to my background in mechanics where precision is vital. My depression fuels a habit of doubting information, while low agreeableness prevents me from blindly accepting viral claims. I use verification methods similar to checking blueprints or technical manuals in engineering, applied here through trusted sources and cross-referencing.
